paragraphs.en: It is Saturday afternoon. Jack wants to call his friend Tom to play football in the park. He picks up his phone and calls Tom's house. Tom's mum answers the phone.
paragraphs.fr: C'est samedi apres-midi. Jack veut appeler son ami Tom pour jouer au football dans le parc. Il prend son telephone et appelle chez Tom. La maman de Tom repond au telephone.

paragraphs.en: Hello, Mrs Parker. This is Jack. Can I speak to Tom, please?
paragraphs.fr: Bonjour, Mme Parker. C'est Jack. Est-ce que je peux parler a Tom, s'il vous plait ?
paragraphs.speaker: Jack

paragraphs.en: Hello, Jack. Hold on, please. I'll get him.
paragraphs.fr: Bonjour, Jack. Attends un instant, s'il te plait. Je vais le chercher.
paragraphs.speaker: Tom's mum

paragraphs.en: Jack waits for a moment. He hears Tom's mum calling Tom. Then Tom comes to the phone.
paragraphs.fr: Jack attend un moment. Il entend la maman de Tom appeler Tom. Puis Tom arrive au telephone.

paragraphs.en: Hi Tom! I'm good, thanks. I'm calling because I want to play football in the park. Do you want to come?
paragraphs.fr: Salut Tom ! Je vais bien, merci. J'appelle parce que je veux jouer au football dans le parc. Tu veux venir ?
paragraphs.speaker: Jack

paragraphs.en: Oh, I'm sorry. I can't come right now. I'm doing my homework. It's very long!
paragraphs.fr: Oh, je suis desole. Je ne peux pas venir maintenant. Je suis en train de faire mes devoirs. C'est tres long !
paragraphs.speaker: Tom

paragraphs.en: That's OK. Can you come later? Maybe at three o'clock?
paragraphs.fr: Pas de probleme. Tu peux venir plus tard ? Peut-etre a trois heures ?
paragraphs.speaker: Jack

paragraphs.en: Yes, three o'clock is perfect! I'll call you back when I'm finished.
paragraphs.fr: Oui, trois heures c'est parfait ! Je te rappellerai quand j'aurai fini.
paragraphs.speaker: Tom

paragraphs.en: Great! Talk to you later. Bye!
paragraphs.speaker: Jack

paragraphs.en: Bye, Jack! See you at three!
paragraphs.speaker: Tom

paragraphs.en: Tom finishes his homework at half past two. He calls Jack back and they meet at the park at three o'clock. They play football for two hours. It is a great afternoon!
paragraphs.fr: Tom finit ses devoirs a deux heures et demie. Il rappelle Jack et ils se retrouvent au parc a trois heures. Ils jouent au football pendant deux heures. C'est un super apres-midi !

vocabulary.definition_en: un appareil utilise pour parler a quelqu'un qui est dans un autre endroit
vocabulary.example: My phone is on the table. Can you pass it to me?
I will call you tomorrow morning.

vocabulary.definition_en: une information ecrite ou parlee envoyee a quelqu'un
vocabulary.example: I sent you a message but you didn't answer.
Can I speak to the manager, please?

vocabulary.definition_en: rester au meme endroit jusqu'a ce que quelque chose se passe
vocabulary.example: Please wait here. I'll be back in a minute.

vocabulary.definition_en: qui a beaucoup de choses a faire
vocabulary.example: Sorry, I'm busy right now. Can I call you later?

vocabulary.definition_en: a un moment dans le futur, pas maintenant
vocabulary.example: I can't talk now. I'll call you later.

vocabulary.definition_en: qui ressent du regret ou de la tristesse a propos de quelque chose
vocabulary.example: I'm sorry, I can't come to the party tonight.

grammar_points.title: 'Can I speak to...?' — Phone Language
grammar_points.explanation: Au telephone, on utilise **'Can I speak to...?'** pour demander a parler a quelqu'un. On dit **'This is [name]'** pour s'identifier (pas 'I am [name]'). Ce sont des expressions figees utilisees au telephone en anglais.
examples: Can I speak to Tom, please? (= Est-ce que je peux parler a Tom ?)
This is Jack. (= C'est Jack. / Jack a l'appareil.)
Who's calling, please? (= Qui est a l'appareil ?)
Hold on, please. (= Attendez, s'il vous plait.)

grammar_points.title: Present Continuous Introduction: 'I'm calling...', 'I'm doing...'
grammar_points.explanation: Le **present continuous** decrit ce qui se passe **en ce moment**. On le forme avec **'am/is/are' + verbe-ing**. Comparez avec le present simple : 'I call Tom every day' (habitude) vs. 'I'm calling Tom now' (en cours maintenant).
examples: I'm calling because I want to play football. (= right now)
I'm doing my homework. (= at this moment)
She's talking on the phone. (= right now)
Compare: I call my mum every day. (habit) vs. I'm calling my mum now. (right now)

expressions.meaning_fr: Est-ce que je peux parler a... ?
expressions.example: Can I speak to Mr Johnson, please?
expressions.note: La facon standard de demander a parler a quelqu'un au telephone. Ajoutez toujours 'please'.

expressions.meaning_fr: Attendez / Patientez un instant, s'il vous plait.
expressions.example: Hold on, please. I'll get her for you.
expressions.note: 'Hold on' signifie 'attends un instant'. Tres courant au telephone.

expressions.example: He's not here right now. I'll call back later.
expressions.note: 'Call back' signifie rappeler quelqu'un. On peut aussi dire 'I'll call you back.'

expressions.meaning_fr: A plus tard ! / On se parle plus tard !
expressions.example: OK, I have to go now. Talk to you later!
expressions.note: Une facon tres courante et amicale de terminer une conversation telephonique.

written.question: Why does Jack call Tom?
options: To play football in the park
written.explanation: Jack says 'I'm calling because I want to play football in the park.'

written.question: Why can't Tom come right now?
written.explanation: Tom says 'I'm doing my homework. It's very long!'

written.question: How do you identify yourself on the phone in English?
written.explanation: On the phone, we say 'This is [name]' (not 'I am [name]'). 'This is Jack' is the correct form.

written.hint: This is the standard phone expression to ask for someone.

written.sentence: I can't talk now. I ________ (do) my homework. I'll call you ________.
written.hint: Use the present continuous for what is happening now. The second word means 'not now, after some time'.

written.french: Bonjour, c'est Jack. Est-ce que je peux parler a Tom ? — Attends un instant, s'il te plait.
written.suggested_answer: Hello, this is Jack. Can I speak to Tom? — Hold on, please.

oral.instruction: Read this phone conversation aloud. Use natural phone language.
oral.text:
Jack: Hello, Mrs Parker. This is Jack. Can I speak to Tom, please?
Tom's mum: Hello, Jack. Hold on, please. I'll get him.
Tom: Hi Jack! How are you?
Jack: I'm calling because I want to play football. Do you want to come?
oral.focus: Use a friendly, polite tone. Practise the phone expressions: 'This is Jack', 'Can I speak to...', 'Hold on, please'.

oral.question: You call a friend to invite them to the cinema. Your friend's dad answers. What do you say?
oral.suggested_answer: Hello, Mr Smith. This is [your name]. Can I speak to Sarah, please? ... Hi Sarah! I'm calling because I want to go to the cinema tonight. Do you want to come?

oral.question: Your friend calls you, but you are busy. Explain what you are doing and suggest calling back later.
oral.suggested_answer: Hi! I'm sorry, I can't talk right now. I'm doing my homework. Can I call you back later? Maybe at five o'clock?
